messages.text: What custom tool would you like to build for this space? (e.g., Kanban board, project dashboard, asset tracker)
build a kanban board t otrack the teams work

**Defining Scope for the Kanban**

I'm now zeroing in on the project's parameters. The instructions emphasize strict scope discipline, prioritizing simplicity above all else. My focus is laser-targeted on building only what's explicitly requested: a Kanban board. I'm deliberately avoiding any feature creep or unnecessary complexity to align with the "radical simplicity" principle.


**Refining Board Simplicity**

I'm now implementing the Kanban with extreme restraint. My design includes the core three columns (To Do, In Progress, Done) and plain text cards, resisting additional UI features. There's a rule about populating the board with initial tasks and real user data when the request involves tasks or people, so I'm populating with our workspace team members: Ajay Yadav, and Belinda Preno, using their real names and avatar photo URLs.


**Populating Kanban with Tasks**

I'm now populating the Kanban board. I've added Mirjam van Esch and Simon Sok, with their details. Context reveals this is for Project Ollie, so I'll create tasks for that project: "Implement ScaNN index for vector similarity search" and "Set up capability-based routing in Task service" will be the initial tasks. The pre-populated tasks are designed to be realistic and relevant.


**Implementing Collaboration and Style**

I'm now integrating real-time collaboration with the Kanban, leveraging short-polling for state synchronization. I've chosen `/api/sync/:envId/ollie_kanban_board` as the endpoint for state persistence. I am now applying the Polaris/M3 design system for the user interface; Google Fonts are embedded, and a minimalistic card layout with rounded avatars and no gradients are now in place. I have carefully selected M3 tokens to establish a clean, consistent aesthetic.


**Defining Style Tokens**

I'm now focusing on style tokens for consistent UI elements. I'm setting CSS variables for spacing and padding, specifically for icons, side panels, and headers. The `gemini-` prefix is used, and I've established values for gaps and paddings within these components. The aim is to create a clean, modern aesthetic. I've noted a need to output the completed application code to the chat in markdown blocks.


**Confirming No Local File Output**

I've just confirmed the critical instruction: absolutely no local file writing; everything goes directly to the chat as markdown code. I'm focusing the UI design and features: I will build a simple Kanban layout with the "Project Ollie Kanban Board" title/logo. I'll include the three columns ("To Do", "In Progress", "Done"), each with an "+ Add task" button. Clicking the button opens a simple inline form for task input (title and assignee). Each card will feature the task title and the assignee's avatar (with name on hover/tooltip), and arrow buttons to move the cards.


**Prioritizing Board Interaction**

I'm now focusing on user interactions within the Kanban board. I've decided to implement HTML5 drag-and-drop for intuitive card movement. I'm also adding fallback arrow buttons for accessibility and mobile support, ensuring ease of use. The aim is to create a clean and reliable interaction experience. I've populated the board with the team members' information and associated tasks.


**Synchronizing the Kanban State**

I'm now integrating the synchronization script to maintain board state. I'll use `POST /api/sync/:envId/:key` and `GET /api/sync/:envId/:key` for data updates. I'm ensuring input/textarea focus persists during short-polling to avoid any disruption. I'll add the new tasks "Integrate Gemini 2.5 Pro with Sobi Harness cognitive loop" assigned to Belinda Preno (In Progress), and "Configure ingestion listeners for Buganizer and Critique webhooks" assigned to Mirjam van Esch (In Progress), and the tasks already done, "Establish logical boundaries in Memory DB across four tiers" assigned to Mirjam van Esch and "Set up Google Wiz UI framework for primary workspace dashboard" assigned to Ajay Yadav. I'm focusing on writing precise synchronization logic. I'm implementing Tailwind CSS, carefully overriding M3 colors, setting background to `#ffffff` for cards, and surface container to `#f0f4f9` for board columns.


**Refining the UI's Design**

I'm now refining the UI with meticulous attention to detail. I'm opting for clean, semantic CSS or direct inline styles to maximize precision, prioritizing absolute control within the M3 Design System. The implementation will mirror the aesthetic of native Google Workspace apps like Keep or Tasks, with a focus on a Google Sans font and the Polaris design. I'm focusing on defining CSS variables in a `<style>` block and mapping them to utility classes, and this approach is the fastest and simplest to implement.


**Defining Board Styles**

I'm now detailing the visual styling of the Kanban board, starting with the overall layout. I'm focusing on a flexbox-based structure with three equally-sized columns, using `#f0f4f9` for the column background and `#ffffff` for the card backgrounds, per the M3 design. I'm prioritizing Google Sans, with specified font sizes and weights for headers and card text.


**Implementing Card Details**

I'm now detailing card elements for the Kanban. Each card will now feature a flex layout at the bottom, showcasing the assignee's rounded avatar along with their name in small text, using 12px font size and a weight of 400. To simplify interactions, I'll add both intuitive drag-and-drop functionality and hover-activated arrow buttons for column movement. I'm also implementing a new, clean "Add Task" button with a simple inline input form to avoid unnecessary complexity. The form includes a text input for task description and a dropdown to select one of the four team members for task assignment.


**Defining Task Assignee Structures**

I'm now determining data structures. Since tasks are to be assigned to workspace team members, I'm setting up an array `TEAM_MEMBERS` with relevant details for each assignee, including name, email, and avatar URL. This will enable streamlined assignment and realistic task population on the board, maintaining strict adherence to scope discipline.


**Designing the Kanban Data**

I'm now establishing the Kanban board's data structures, specifically for task synchronization. I've defined a `GET` and `POST` API with the sync key `ollie_kanban_board_state` and the data object will contain tasks, each with an `id`, `title`, `assignee`, and `column` for synchronization. I'm focusing on simplicity and direct interactions. I'm also planning to incorporate a robust synchronizer using standard `fetch` calls. I'm prioritizing data integrity in the Kanban board.


**Finalizing Visual Consistency**

I'm now establishing final visual consistency. I'm focusing on a clean, modern interface with distinct typography and spacing. The Kanban board will feature a prominent header displaying "Project Ollie Task Board" in bold text, accompanied by a small workspace identifier. I've defined M3 color tokens for consistency, using those to set background and surface colors, as well as the text contrast.


**Defining Board States**

I'm now detailing the board's interactive elements and states. I am implementing a tag indicator for the active workspace, and also a clear reload/sync indicator. I'm focusing on three columns with titles, task counts and the inline task creators, to include a title area/input, and an assignee selector for each task. Empty states will show "No tasks", and I'm designing drag-and-drop actions for easy column transitions, with visual highlights. Edit and delete actions will also be included for each card.


**Implementing UI Polishing**

I'm now integrating premium visual elements. I've focused on Google-style icon buttons for delete, arrows, and add functions, choosing clean SVG icons. I've embedded SVGs for a checkmark, trash bin, left/right arrows, a plus icon, and a user avatar placeholder. I'm focusing on preserving active input and dropdown selections during state synchronization, preserving the `document.activeElement` and `selectionRange` to prevent focus loss during updates.


**Refining State Synchronization**

I'm now implementing smart rendering for Kanban updates, only refreshing the DOM when the synced state differs from the local one, while preserving active input states. I'll compare the local and server tasks with a `JSON.stringify` comparison. If different, I will re-render just the tasks list containers of each column, not the add task form containers. This ensures the add form state will persist across syncs.


**Constructing the HTML Structure**

I'm now building the full HTML structure. The focus is a complete `index.html` block, including the document head with embedded Google Fonts. I'll include the necessary HTML elements, ensuring a clean and functional foundation. My primary focus is to adhere to the given instructions, optimizing the Kanban board's architecture for drag-and-drop and intuitive user interactions, including persistence of form data.


**Defining Default Kanban Tasks**

I'm now generating default tasks for the Kanban. The existing tasks I'll be adding are: "Implement ScaNN vector indexing in Spanner for Tasks" to Ajay Yadav, and "Design secure credential propagation mechanism for Jetski" for Simon Sok. I'm also including Belinda Preno's task "Integrate Gemini 2.5 Pro with Sobi Harness cognitive loop".


**Finalizing Synchronization Logic**

I'm now incorporating detailed syncing logic to the design, focusing on data updates through a REST API and real-time UI updates with local task comparison, while maintaining input states to prevent disruption and keep a smooth user experience. The Kanban board will include tasks such as: "Implement ScaNN vector indexing in Spanner for Tasks" for Ajay Yadav, "Design secure credential propagation mechanism for Jetski" for Simon Sok, and the existing task for Belinda Preno, "Integrate Gemini 2.5 Pro with Sobi Harness cognitive loop". I'm validating the project, team members and details.


**Implementing Card Functionality**

I'm now implementing the card functionality, focusing on drag-and-drop interactions to allow intuitive task movement between columns. I'll utilize HTML5's `draggable="true"` and corresponding drag events to handle `dragstart`, `dragover`, `dragenter`, `dragleave`, `drop`, and `dragend`, which will enable task relocation through these actions. I'll maintain a standard `<select>` for assignee selection in the inline input form, which offers robustness while still allowing inline avatar presentation within rendered cards.


**Adding Card Navigation**

I'm now integrating arrow buttons and delete functionality to each card for improved interaction. I'll implement chevron buttons (left and right) for column transitions, based on the card's current status and an intuitive delete function, using a small trash bin SVG icon at the top right of each card.


**Validating M3 and Sync Logic**

I'm now integrating Murdock's Polaris/M3 specification: Google Sans fonts, borders, backgrounds and primary colors are prioritized. I've built the local state synchronizer, which loads states from local storage for local development environments, otherwise loading tasks from the server.


**Implementing Synchronization Logic**

I'm now integrating the provided synchronization logic, focusing on data updates through REST API calls and UI updates with local task comparison. The Kanban board will now load from server or from local storage, based on the `envId`, with fallback to default tasks. I've validated this project, team members and details. The data is synchronized on the server.


**Refining Initial Task Structure**

I'm now refining the initial task structure for the Kanban board. I've updated the `getDefaultTasks` function to reflect real email addresses for assignees and more clearly define the task columns. My primary focus is on ensuring these default tasks accurately populate the Kanban board with realistic and relevant data. I'm also ensuring the synchronization process includes a fallback to local storage for a smooth development and testing experience.


**Adding Task Edit Interactions**

I'm now incorporating inline task editing. Clicking a task's title now triggers an inline input field, replacing the text. Changes are saved on "Enter" or when the input loses focus. I'm focusing on simplicity and premium quality with minimal visual noise, enhancing the board's user-friendliness.


**Structuring the UI Styles**

I'm now establishing the core structure for the UI styles. I'm building CSS variables, beginning with Polaris/M3 design tokens to ensure a modern and consistent design. I'm also preparing to create a modular layout using utility classes for common styles, optimizing speed and maintainability.

The Kanban board will now load from server or from local storage, based on the `envId`, with fallback to default tasks. I've validated this project, team members and details. The data is synchronized on the server.

**Refining Initial Task Structure**

I'm now refining the initial task structure for the Kanban board. I've updated the `getDefaultTasks` function to reflect real email addresses for assignees and more clearly define the task columns. My primary focus is on ensuring these default tasks accurately populate the Kanban board with realistic and relevant data. I'm also ensuring the synchronization process includes a fallback to local storage for a smooth development and testing experience.

**Adding Task Edit Interactions**

I'm now incorporating inline task editing. Clicking a task's title now triggers an inline input field, replacing the text. Changes are saved on "Enter" or when the input loses focus. I'm focusing on simplicity and premium quality with minimal visual noise, enhancing the board's user-friendliness.

**Structuring CSS with Variables**

I'm now establishing a structured, modular approach to CSS using variables. I am creating a system of CSS variables from the Polaris/M3 design tokens to create a modern and consistent design with the CSS provided. I am using utility classes, optimizing maintainability, which is vital for quick iteration, as I build.

**Finalizing Form Interactions**

I'm now putting the finishing touches on the task editing interactions. Clicking a card title activates the inline input for edits and I have set the form to disappear on "Enter" or blur. I'm focusing on ensuring visual feedback is minimal to maintain an elegant look. I'm meticulously testing the edit workflow for both title and form.

**Defining UI Variables**

I'm now integrating the provided CSS structure with defined variables to establish a consistent, modern UI. I've focused on mapping Polaris/M3 design tokens to the application's visual elements using variables and utility classes. I'm focusing on seamless task editing, optimizing the workflow.

**Refining Task Editing Styles**

I'm now refining the inline task editing experience. Implementing CSS to highlight editable task titles and focus the input field upon click is my current focus. I am implementing this approach with minimal visual noise to promote a seamless, elegant workflow.

**Implementing Task Input Forms**

I'm now implementing the task input forms with refined aesthetics, with the goal of creating clear interactions. I'm focusing on CSS styles for editable task titles and focus the input field upon clicking, which will establish an elegant workflow.

**Adding Card Navigation**

I'm now integrating arrow buttons and delete functionality to each card. I will implement left/right chevron buttons for column transitions, and an intuitive delete function. I am focusing on making the chevron button functionality for moving cards based on current column and also incorporating a delete function.

```html
<div class="card-actions">
  <button class="action-btn move-left-btn" title="Move Left" onclick="moveCard('${task.id}', -1)">
    <svg width="16" height="16" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><path d="M15 19l-7-7 7-7"/></svg>
  </button>
  <button class="action-btn move-right-btn" title="Move Right" onclick="moveCard('${task.id}', 1)">
    <svg width="16" height="16" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke
```

**Implementing Card Navigation and Actions**

I'm now implementing the `moveCard()` and `deleteCard()` functions. I've incorporated arrow button and delete functionality to each card, and have defined the logic for `moveCard(id, direction)` with state saving and re-rendering to reflect the new state of the Kanban. I have also added inline editing functionality for card titles.

**Refining Edit Interaction**

I'm now implementing inline editing with `contenteditable="true"` for simplicity, using `onblur` and `onkeydown` for elegant handling. I've designed drag interactions, hiding chevrons to ensure visual consistency during drag operations, focusing on the seamless UX.

**Implementing Drag-and-Drop UX**

I'm now integrating HTML5 drag-and-drop for tasks. I've built robust drag event handlers (`dragstart`, `dragover`, `dragenter`, `dragleave`, `drop`) to facilitate seamless, intuitive card movement between columns, along with CSS class changes for visual feedback. My focus is a smooth user experience.

**Finalizing Add Task Form**

I'm now completing the add task form implementation. I am integrating an add card button with an inline form for task details and assignee selection, and the forms now render in the UI. I have also added `cancel` and `add` buttons, and I am focused on the seamless integration with existing functionality.

**Refining Form Integration**

I'm now integrating the form into each column. I've focused on seamless inline forms and button interactions, ensuring smooth transitions within the Kanban board. The form structure is optimized for elegant interactions and is ready for use, with validation and data handling fully in place.

**Optimizing UX and Rendering**

I'm now focusing on optimizing UI updates. I'm strategically re-rendering columns without disrupting the task forms. I've ensured team member avatars are accurately displayed on cards. This will create a flawless real-time multiplayer board.

**Finalizing Board Interactivity**

I'm now implementing the reload button next to the workspace name, and also refining the state synchronization indicator. I've confirmed real-time synchronization with local fallback, and the entire code base is self-contained. The code, meeting all criteria, is ready for output.

sandboxFiles.name: Ollie
sandboxFiles.content:
# Presentation: Ollie

Type: Google Slides
File ID: 13fWjGKeE-Uq2ZPpdnXU1xFkvA5Jltiic0HJ8UpDsNgE

sandboxFiles.name: [NTK] Project Ollie Eng Design
[UX Research plan] Project Ollie: Baseline Workflow & Agent Delegation
sandboxFiles.content:
Core Objective
To map the data trails, tool ecosystems, and context-sharing habits of knowledge workers in the real world, as well as their desires and expectations regarding AI delegation.
Participants
* Location & Availability: Must currently live in the U.S. and be able to attend a 90-minute, in-person study session at the NYC Google Office between July 6th and July 24th with their own laptop.1
* Company Profile: Employed at a private educational institute or a commercial business/organization (excluding public education, nonprofits, and government).1
* Software & Tool Usage:
   * Their company must use Google Workspace (Gmail, Drive, Docs, or Calendar) with official work email addresses.1
   * The "actual work" they do must involve co-editing shared documents and spreadsheets (such as Google Docs or Sheets). Users who primarily manage tasks in dedicated project management tools (like Jira, Asana, or Trello) are disqualified.1
* Role & Authority: Must have a role in their business's decision to purchase digital tools and software (either as a sole decision-maker, part of a collective group, or as an influencer).1
* Collaboration Level: They must spend at least half or more of their work week collaborating with others on projects, tasks, or shared goals.1
Details here: Ollie current needs and painpoints 


Research Questions 
* RQ1 (The Fragmented Truth): What does a knowledge worker's true "to-do list" actually look like across formal tools (Jira, Asana) and informal channels (Slack stars, open browser tabs, mental notes)?
* RQ2 (Context Breadcrumbs): When a user moves a task forward, what specific artifacts (links, documents, specific chat sentences) represent the "proof of work" that Ollie will eventually need to ingest?
* RQ3 (The Translation Gap): How do users naturally summarize a complex day of work into a project update? What details do they filter out, and what do they consider critical?
* RQ4 (Agent Delegation Triggers): What tasks do users actually want to hand off versus what they want to keep control over? What parameters (triggers, boundaries, outputs) define a successful Sojo Agent assignment?
Session script
Artifact & Task Audit (15 mins)
* Goal: Uncover the hidden, informal task ecosystems.
* Activity: Have them open their laptop and show you their current "active stack."
* Prompts to use:
   * "Show me the 3 things you know you have to do today but aren't written down in a formal project board."
   * "Look at your open browser tabs or flagged emails right now—what tasks do those represent?"
   * "Where is the conversation happening about your top priority right now? Can you show me that Slack thread/email chain?"
The "Human Ollie" Walkthrough (25 mins)
* Goal: Treat the user's memory and screen as the database to see what Ollie should have done.
* Activity: Walk backward through their last 24–48 hours of work. Have them literally point at things on their screen.
* Prompts to use:
   * "You opened this Google Doc at 10 AM and left a comment. If Ollie was a smart assistant watching this, what is the 'core project update' it should extract from that action?"
   * "If an AI wrote an update based purely on this Slack thread you just showed me, what would it miss? What's the unwritten context here?"
   * "Look at this completed task. What were all the scattered pieces of data (spreadsheets, links, client feedback) that were required to get it done?"
AI Agents tasks (15 mins)
* Goal: Use their real data to define the scope and boundaries of the agents.
* Activity: Review the real tasks identified in Phase 1 and 2. Print out blank "Sojo Agent Job Description" cards (or use a digital whiteboard) and fill them out together.
* Prompts to use:
   * "Look at this specific task you spent an hour on yesterday. If an AI Agent could have done it, what is the exact trigger to start it? (e.g., 'When a new CSV lands in this folder' or 'When a client pings me via email')."
   * "What does the agent need access to on your laptop to successfully do this?"
   * "What is the exact output you expect? A draft email? A spreadsheet update? And where should it put it?"
